title: Switching From 'Unipolar' to Bipolar II
abstract:
Switching From 'Unipolar' to Bipolar II
An 11-Year Prospective Study of Clinical and Temperamental Predictors
in 559 Patients
Hagop S. Akiskal, MD; Jack D. Maser, PhD; Pamela J. Zeller, PhD; Jean Endicott, PhD; William Coryell, MD;
Martin Keller, MD; Meredith Warshaw, MS; Paula Clayton, MD; Frederick Goodwin, MD
Background: Given the therapeutic and prognostic im-
portance of the unipolar-bipolar dichotomy, predicting
which patients will become bipolar subsequent to index
diagnosis of major depressive disorder (MDD) is of Para-
mount clinical significance. We sought to characterize
the profile of patients with MDD who would convert to
the more subtle bipolar subtype (known as BPII) on the
basis of clinical and personality variables obtained dur-
ing MDD episodes.
Methods: A total of 559 patients, comprehensively evalu-
ated with the Schedule ofAffective Disorders and Schizo-
phrenia and "unipolar" MDD at entry, were adminis-
tered 17 self-report personality measures. Hypomanic and
manic episodes were systematically recorded over a pro-
spective observation period of up to 11 years. We com-
pared 48 converters to BPII (8.6%) with 22 converters
to bipolar I (BPI) (3.9%) and the remaining larger group
of unipolar patients.
Results: Except for greater acuteness, severity, and psy-
chotic symptomatology, BPI converters were essentially
similar to MDD nonconverters. By contrast, BPII con-
verters were robustly distinguished from those with MDD
who remained unipolar on the basis of self-report mea-
sures along the newly derived factors of Mood Lability,
Energy-Activity, and Daydreaming. This profile was as-
sociated with early age at onset of MDD and pleomor-
phic psychopathology beyond the usual affective realm,
high rates of substance abuse, as well as educational, mari-
tal, and occupational disruption and minor antisocial acts
prior to discrete hypomanic episodes. Overall, BPII switch-
ers had a more protracted and tempestuous course with
shorter well intervals. "Habitual self" descriptions of tem-
peramental instability during MDD episodes provided use-
ful clinical information for predicting which depressed
patients will switch to BPII, attaining a sensitivity of 91%
for all three factors combined (23 items); Mood Lability
alone (nine items) was the most specific predictor (86%),
though of lower sensitivity (42%).
Conclusions: The BPII subtype is best understood by such
lability intruding into, and possibly its accentuation dur-
ing, depressive episodes, thereby creating an intimate in-
terweaving of trait and state. Clinicians must note that the
foregoing temperamental profile appears more fundamen-
tal in defining the affective dysregulation of the BPII sub-
type than hypomanic episodes emphasized in DSM-IV.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1995;52:114-123)
The
switch process (ie, hy¬
pomanic or manic shifts in
clinically depressed pa¬
tients) and the broader
phenomenon of bipolar
conversion over the course of major de¬
pressive disorder (MDD) are fascinating
from a psychobiologic perspective.1,2 Given
the therapeutic and prognostic signifi¬
cance of the unipolar-bipolar dichotomy,
predicting which patients will become bi¬
polar subsequent to an index diagnosis of
MDD is of considerable clinical signifi¬
cance. Much past research has focused on
conversions of MDD to full-blown manic
depressive or bipolar I (BPI) disorders.3"8
Rates have varied from 0%8 to 37.5%4 (me¬
dian, 9.7%). Bipolar family history in con¬
secutive generations, adolescent or early
adult age at onset, and acute onset of epi¬
sodes with stupor or psychotic features
have been reported to distinguish con¬
verters from nonconverters.5'6 These vari¬
ables, which were tested more recently by
Coryell et al,9 were found to be of rela¬
tively modest clinical utility because of
their very low sensitivity; moreover, their
main relevance is for BPI conversion.
Because of the difficulties of ascer¬
taining hypomania by history,10"13 conver¬
sion to bipolar II (BPII) disorder presents
the greater challenge for the clinician. This
From the National Institute of
Mental Health (NIMH)
Collaborative Program on the
Psychobiology of Depression
(Clinical Studies)
(Drs Akiskal, Maser, Endicott,
Coryell, Keller, Clayton, and
Goodwin and Ms Warshaw)
and DataMax, Inc, Rockville,
Md (Dr Zeller). For other
members of the NIMH
Collaborative Program on the
Psychobiology of Depression,
see box at the end of the
article.
Downloaded From: http://archpsyc.jamanetwork.com/ by a Princeton University User  on 07/19/2013
 METHODS
THE COLLABORATIVE DEPRESSION STUDY
The data reported are drawn from the National Institute
of Mental Health (NIMH) Collaborative Depression Study
(CDS), a longitudinal, prospective investigation of the phe¬
nomenology, nosology, familial aggregation, and course in
955 moderately to severely ill affective probands seeking
treatment at one of five university centers. As described else¬
where,19"21 the probands were predominantly inpatients
(79.7%); all were white, English speaking, at least 17 years
old, knowledgeable about both biological parents, had an
IQ of at least 70, and were without evidence of concurrent
organic mental disorder or terminal illness.
The subjects were comprehensively evaluated for cur¬
rent and past history of mental disorders using the Re¬
search Diagnostic Criteria (RDC),22 based on the Sched¬
ule of Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (SADS).23
Prospective data were gathered at 6-month intervals for 5
years and yearly thereafter, through 11 years at this writ¬
ing, via the Longitudinal Interval Follow-up Evaluation21
or variations of that form designed for less frequent follow-
up. Information from psychiatric records was routinely in¬
corporated into the patients' intake and follow-up inter¬
views, thereby ensuring a record of hypomania (and/or
mania) if or when it occurred.
PERSONALITY BATTERY
Patients and controlscompleted a 436-itemself-report person¬
alitybattery24 thatconsisted of 17 conventionalpersonality scales
thought to be relevant to affective disorders: the General Ac¬
tivity, Restraint, Ascendance, Sociability, Emotional Stability,
Objectivity, and Thoughtfulness scales of the Guilford-
Zimmerman Temperament Survey (GZTS)25; the Emotional
Reliance on Another Person, Lack of Social Self-confidence,
and Assertion ofAutonomyscales ofthe Interpersonal Depen¬
dency Inventory26; the Orality, Obsessionality, and Hysteri¬
cal Pattern scalesoftheshort form ofthe Lazare-Klerman-Armor
Personality Inventory27; the Neuroticism and Extraversion-
Introversion scales of the Maudsley Personality Inventory28;
and the Ego Resiliency and Ego Control-5 scales of the Min¬
nesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI).W
Previous reports from the CDS30 32 have reported per¬
sonality measures organized into groups of scales based on
their theoretical similarities (eg, emotional strength, inter¬
personal dependency). For the present article, the 17 scales
have been organized into five groups according to their fac¬
torial structure (using principal components factor analysis
with varimax rotations). By this new analysis (PJ.Z. et al, un¬
published data, 1993), seven scales constitute the first group:
Neuroticism, Objectivity, Ego Resiliency, Emotional Stabil¬
ity, Orality, Emotional Reliance on Another Person, and Lack
of Social Self-confidence. A second group consists of Extra-
version, Sociability, and Ascendance. A third group consists
ofThoughtfulness and Restraint and a fourth subsumes Ego
Control-5 and Obsessionality. Scales measuring Hysterical Pat¬
tern, Assertion of Autonomy, and General Activity did not
share common variance with any other scales and so are con¬
sidered alone. The first four of these factors are reminiscent
of similar factors in two new widely acclaimed and empiri¬
cally derived personality systems.33·34
IDENTIFICATION OF BIPOLAR CONVERSION
IN MDD PATIENTS
Of the 955 patients who entered the CDS (Table I ), 730
had definite MDD at intake with no evidence of schizo¬
phrenia or schizoaffective disorder (at any time during fol¬
low-up). Ninety-six patients with mania prior to intake (BPI)
and 75 with hypomania but no mania prior to intake (BPII)
were further excluded.
For the remaining 559 patients with MDD defined as
having unipolar illness ("unipolar") as of their entry into
the study, all available follow-up data were examined to
identify those whose first lifetime RDC diagnosis of mania
or hypomania developed during the course of prospective
follow-up. Mania developed in 22 patients (3.9%) and they
were reclassified as BPI converters; hypomania developed
first in eight of these 22 and then mania. Hypomania, but
no mania, developed in 48 (8.6%) during follow-up (BPII
converters). This left 489 index patients with unipolar MDD
(87.5%) who remained unipolar throughout follow-up, and
provide a comparison to the two groups of converters con¬
sidered in this article. Because of our focus on BPII, schi¬
zoaffective cases were excluded, which is the main reason
Continued on next page
disorder is characterized by severe depressions, typi¬
cally recurrent, and milder hypomanias of at least a few
days' duration. As a result, unless expertly questioned
about hypomania, the bipolar nature of these depres¬
sions can be missed.
Coryell et al9 reported that 7.2% ofpatients with non-
bipolar MDD converted to BPII; young age and pro¬
tracted depressive episodes were the main predictors. The
present article focuses on prospective predictors of BPII
switching from the vantage of a broader array of clini¬
cal, anamnestic, and personality parameters, most ofwhich
have not been tested in previous research; data on BPI
conversion are presented primarily for comparison. Most
important, we undertook these analyses in an effort to
characterize self-report trait measures by which de¬
pressed patients, who might ultimately be classified as
BPII, describe themselves. Such self-description is par-
ticularly relevant in that the BPII subtype is often con¬
sidered to have lifelong instability with cyclothymic and/or
labile mood shifts but without necessarily meeting dis¬
crete criteria for hypomanic episodes.1415 We specifi¬
cally set out to characterize such temperamental factors
that clinical observation16 18 has linked to "soft" bipolar
expressions. Finally, we wished to examine the predic¬
tive power of such self-rated temperamental measures rela¬
tive to observer-rated variables.
RESULTS
COURSE AND EPISODE CHARACTERISTICS
OF BIPOLAR CONVERSION
Although 20 of 48 onsets of first BPII disorder (41.7%),
compared with six of 22 onsets of first BPI disorder

present article focuses on prospective predictors of BPII

conversion are presented primarily for comparison. Most

pressed patients, who might ultimately be classified as

sidered to have lifelong instability with cyclothymic and/or

crete criteria for hypomanic episodes.1415 We specifi¬

that clinical observation16 18 has linked to "soft" bipolar
expressions. Finally, we wished to examine the predic¬

COURSE AND EPISODE CHARACTERISTICS
Although 20 of 48 onsets of first BPII disorder (41.7%),
compared with six of 22 onsets of first BPI disorder
for minor variation in the rates ofbipolar conversion com¬
pared with an earlier CDS anaysis by Coryell et al9 (8.6%
A high percentage (32.9%) ofbipolar converters, eight
of22 BPI and 15 of48 BPII, began theirfirst lifetime hypomanic
or manic episodes during the first 6 months of prospective
follow-up. Thereafter, conversion to bipolarity was spread as
follows: five cases between 6 months and 1 year offollow-up
(7.1%), 10 in the second year offollow-up (14.3%), 23 in the
third to fifth years (32.9%), and nine in the sixth to ninth years
offollow-up (12.9%). Thus, in line with previous data,6,9 much
of bipolar conversion took place in the first 5 years.
Because past research5-6-9 has shown that different vari¬
ables would distinguish bipolar converters from the group
who remained unipolar, separate  2 comparisons to MDD
were performed for each of the two converting groups on
gender, age, marital status, and education, total number of
lifetime episodes of MDD and nonaffective diagnoses, in¬
dices ofseverity of the intake episode, SADS items and scores
describing the index MDD episode, and SADS items per¬
taining to occupational-educational adjustment since age
15 years. To complement clinician-evaluated psychopa-
thology, self-reported symptoms for the week preceding in¬
take and standard scores from the 90-item Hopkins Symp¬
tom Checklist-90 (SCL-90)35 were subjected to analyses of
variance, covarying for age and gender.
ANALYSIS OF PERSONALITY VARIABLES
This article goes beyond an analysis of the scores of pub¬
lished scales24 to delineate the personality profile of bipo¬
lar converters based on a regrouping of436 individual items
of the self-report battery. To minimize the state effects of
depression on personality,30 (1) subjects were instructed
to ignore their current affective disorder and describe their
usual or "habitual self," and (2) the personality battery was
administered at the index episode after severe symptoms
had abated. Seven additional patients were eliminated from
further analyses because their personality assessment was
subsequent to their bipolar conversion leaving 20 BPI and
43 BPII converters as the focus of our analyses.
Because past research16"18 has related certain tempera¬
mental traits to soft bipolar disorder such as BPII, we hy¬
pothesized that item analyses of certain hypomanic or hy-
perthymic traits might shed additional light beyond scale
score differences. To do so, we first identified those over¬
all scale scores significantly differentiating BPII convert¬
ers from unipolar patients; items were included if the dif¬
ference in adjusted means between the groups (after
covarying for age and gender) was statistically significant
(P<.05). Perusal of many items from various scales of the
separate instruments in the battery suggested greater com¬
monality than is represented by the conventional names
of the scales. For instance, "I have had periods when I felt
so full of pep that sleep did not seem necessary for days at
a time" is an MMPI Ego Resiliency scale item, yet it has face
validity as a measure ofActivity-Energy (GZTS), with dis¬
To identify the dimensions most relevant to bipolar
conversion, principal components factor analyses were
performed on 86 items of the personality battery that
significantly differentiated BPII from BPI converters. All
patients' index personality batteries were used in the
factor analysis, regardless of their psychiatric status at
the time of personality assessment. Five-factor and
14-factor solutions were selected based on examination
of the eigenvalues; factors were rotated by the varimax
method, which yields maximally independent factors.
Factor scores were generated, based on factor weights
for the 86 items, and analyses of variance were per¬
formed on the resulting factor scores to compare BPI
and BPII converters and unipolar subjects, covarying for
To examine the power of index clinical and self-
report variables to predict conversion to BPII, logistic re¬
gression analyses were performed (SAS LOGISTIC proce¬
dure, SAS Institute Ine, Cary, NC) on those variables that
had discriminated BPII from unipolar subjects at P<.05 by
covariate analysis of variance or  2 test. The sensitivity and
specificity of each of the three most powerful sets of dis¬
criminators then were computed for determining their util¬
ity as clinical "test" predictors ofBPII switching. In this con¬
text, sensitivity refers to the percentage of MDD patients
with the variable who have switched to BPII, and specific¬
ity to the percentage of nonswitchers without the vari¬
(27.3%), occurred when the subjects had not yet recov¬
ered from the intake episode, this difference was not sta¬
tistically significant. However, BPI converters recov¬
ered from their intake episodes in significantly less than
half the time (mean, 24.3 weeks) than did BPII or uni¬
polar patients (mean, 52.5 and 59.3 weeks, respec¬
tively; P<.05 by generalized Wilcoxon test of Kaplan-
Meier product-limit estimates of the survivorship
functions). Thus, the index BPI depressive episode was
Because much of bipolar conversion occurred dur¬
ing the first 5 years, we were interested in comparing the
duration ofill vs well periods for the three affective groups.
The two converting groups each had a significantly higher
number of episodes during this period (mean, 4.0 and
3.5 episodes for BPI and BPII converters, respectively)
than the unipolar group (mean, 2.2 episodes; overall
F=27.41, d/=2, P<.001). Furthermore, BPII converters
were ill significantly more than unipolar patients
(mean±SD, 119.6±69.0 vs 90.0±87.0 weeks; t=2.1,
P<.05), which means well interepisodic intervals were
We next sought to examine whether treatment with
heterocyclic antidepressants could account for bipolar
conversion. This possibility is unlikely because during
the month prior to switching, fewer BPI and BPII con¬
verters received heterocyclic antidepressants compared
with unipolar probands in a comparable period, ie, their
first month of prospective follow-up (40.9%, 28.9%, and
Bipolar I converters had more severe index episodes
than unipolar patients as indicated by the greater likeli¬
hood of their being hospitalized (95.4% vs 76.9%,  2=4.19,
P=.041). They also had significantly lower Global Assess-
ment Scale scores (indicative of more impaired function¬
ing) in the week prior to the index assessment (36.9±8.7
vs 43.4± 11.5, t=3.30, P<.001). Bipolar I converters were
rated by clinicians as being significantly worse than uni¬
polar patients on individual SADS items that pertained to
feelings of inadequacy and discouragement, psychic anxi¬
ety, inability to concentrate, social withdrawal, and im¬
paired functioning, as well as delusions and/or hallucina¬
tions (all P<.05). No SCL-90 scores differentiated BPI
converters from those who remained unipolar. The same
was true for all 17 of the personality scales.
CLINICAL CORRELATES OF BPII CONVERSION
Bipolar II converters andunipolar probands were similar in
termsofgender (68.8% and 59.1% female, respectively). How¬
ever, they were significantly younger than subjects who re¬
mained unipolar (average age of 33.7 vs 40.1 years, t=2.84,
P=.005). Converters were less likely to be married or living
with a partner at the time ofadmission to the study (31.2%
vs 50.9%) and were more likely to be separated, widowed,
or divorced (33.3% vs 20.9%) or never married (35.4% vs
28.1%) (overall  2=7.29, P=.03). To control for the possible
effect ofage, marital status was examined among those sub¬
jects who were at least 25 years old at admission, and the
risk ofmarital disruption remained 1.7 times higher for BPII
converters than for unipolar patients.
Bipolar II converters also had a significantly earlier
age of onset at first MDD episode (mean±SD, 25.4± 10.2
vs 31.3+14.3 years, £=2.79, P=.006). In terms of overall
characteristics of their index MDD episode, subjects who
converted to BPII disorder had a significantly longer pe¬
riod of illness prior to entry into the study (median du¬
ration of 142 vs 57 weeks;  2 test for difference of me¬
dians, P=.04). However, converters did not differ
significantly from unipolar patients on primary/
secondary MDD, inpatient/outpatient status, level of over¬
all functioning during the index episode (by Global As¬
sessment Scale), or on the highest level of overall
functioning during the 5 years before admission.
Rates of lifetime RDC diagnoses of nonaffective dis¬
orders were similar for BPII converters and unipolar pa¬
tients, except that converters were more likely to be rated
as ever having RDC cyclothymic personality (8.3% vs
2.9%,  2=4.04, P=.04) or drug use disorder other than
alcohol (22.9% vs 11.4%,  2=5.26, P=.022). Lifetime rates
of alcoholism were similar for the two groups (BPII, 31.2%;
unipolar, 29.2%), and the same was true for anxiety-
related diagnoses (BPII, 18.8%; MDD, 15.1%).
Bipolar II converters were compared with patients
who remained unipolar on individual SADS items and
scores describing the index episode. Converters were sig¬
nificantly more socially withdrawn than unipolar pa¬
tients (52.1% vs 35.4%,  2=5.24, P=.02). Furthermore,
converters were more than twice as likely to have been
rated by the clinician as currently or recently engaging
at least occasionally in minor antisocial acts of a clini¬
cally significant nature (14.6% vs 6.1%;  2=4.86, P=.03),
but they were still well below the diagnostic threshold
for antisocial personality. Significantly more BPII con¬
verters than unipolar patients (37.5% vs 23.9%) were rated
as having shown evidence of poor long-term occupa¬
tional or educational adjustment ( 2=4.28, P=.04), based
on frequent job changes, long periods of unemploy¬
ment, frequent absences from work, or poor academic
This unstable profile is further reflected in pleo-
morphic symptomatology beyond typical affective bound¬
aries. Thus, subjects who became BPII during follow-up
scored significantly worse than unipolar subjects on sum¬
mary SADS scores on Miscellaneous Psychopathology
(mean±SD, 26.2±6.4 vs 24.2±5.8, t=2.06, P=.04) dur¬
ing the index episode. Among nonpsychotic patients, this
last score summarizes responses to SADS items on ob¬
sessions-compulsions, somatic preoccupation, deper-
sonalization, subjective anger, suspiciousness, ideas of
reference, self-pity, and demandingness. The foregoing
clinician-rated complex psychopathology was paral¬
leled by significantly higher self-reported Interpersonal
Sensitivity, Depression, Phobic Anxiety, and Psychoti-
cism scores on the SCL-90 (Table 2) in BPII converters
compared with those subjects who remained unipolar.
PERSONALITY AND BPII CONVERSION
Compared with patients who remained unipolar, BPII con¬
verters scored significantly higher during their index epi¬
sode on scales measuring Neuroticism, Orality, Emo¬
tional Reliance on Another Person, and Lack of Social
Self-confidence, while being significantly lower on scales
measuring Ego Resiliency and Emotional Stability
(Table 3). Objectivity was marginally lower for the BPII
converters (P=.05). The rest of the personality scales were
We next describe four newly derived personality fac¬
tors that differentiated the BPII converters from pa¬
tients who remained unipolar (Table 4).
Mood Lability, combining items from the GZTS Emo¬
tional Stability and MMPI Neuroticism scales, emerged as
the unique discriminating trait of BPII. Thus, the average
weighted factor score for BPII converters was 4-0.69, which
is dramatically higher than the 4-0.11 for unipolar sub¬
jects. Curiously, BPI converters' weighted score on this fac¬
tor (4-0.12) was similar to that of patients with unipolar
*Analysis of variance, covarying for age and sex. Data are given as adjusted mean (SD).
iScores based on response to items describing how much symptom bothered or distressed the patient during the past week or so, with response opinions
as follows: Unot at all; 2=a little bit; 3=moderately; 4=qulte a bit; and 5=extremely.
tOne bipolar II converter and 14 unipolar patients did not complete an intake SCL-90.
The second factor that distinguished BPII convert¬
ers from unipolar MDD subjects is Energy-Activity. Five
items of the GZTS General Activity scale define this fac¬
tor, which also includes items from three other person¬
ality instruments. It is noteworthy that the GZTS Gen¬
eral Activity scale did not significantly differentiate the
two groups, yet the new factor indicating h/perener-
getic involvement in activities did (average factor score
of 4-0.32 for BPII vs —0.20 for unipolar). Bipolar I con¬
verters' average score of —0.01 on this factor indicates
Items pertaining to the third factor, Daydreaming,
come from three different GZTS scales, plus the Maudsley
Personality Inventory Neuroticism and MMPI Ego Re¬
siliency scales. Together they comprise a set of items that
significantly differentiated BPII converters from unipo¬
lar subjects (mean factor scores of 4-0.34 and —0.07, re¬
spectively). Bipolar I converters had a low positive score
The fourth factor that differentiated BPII converters
from unipolar subjects is related to insecurity concerning
social encounters (Social Anxiety), and is defined by sev¬
eral items from the Interpersonal Dependency Inventory
Lack of Social Self-confidence scale, with additional items
from four other scales within the personality battery. Bi¬
polar II converters averaged 4-0.35 on the weighted factor
score for this dimension, compared with —0.01 for uni¬
polar patients, and —0.16 for BPI converters.
RELATIVE PREDICTIVE POWER OF CLINICAL
The unique predictive power of each variable, statisti¬
cally controlling for all the other variables, was deter¬
mined by a logistic regression that considered all the vari¬
ables together. Of the different types ofpredictors analyzed
herein, three of four of the newly derived measures of
self-reported usual personality (Mood Lability, Energy-
Activity, and Daydreaming) each had stronger predic¬
tive power for identifying BPII converters than any other
variable considered. Three of the SCL-90 scores, reflect¬
ing self-reported Phobic Anxiety, Depression, and Inter¬
personal Sensitivity, were the next best predictors of BPII
conversion; these were followed by the newly derived per¬
sonality factor of Social Anxiety, and the SCL-90 Psy-
The power of each set of the foregoing predictors was
further evaluated through separate logistic regression analy¬
ses using the three subsets of variables: clinical descrip¬
tors, self-reported symptoms, and self-reported personal¬
ity (Table 5). Among the clinical predictors, age and the
SADS score summarizing Miscellaneous Psychopathol¬
ogy were the only two that entered the model, indicating
that the predictive power of all other clinical descriptors
was captured in these two variables. In an analysis based
on self-reported symptoms alone, the SCL-90 scores rep¬
resenting Phobic Anxiety and Depression entered the model,
with Interpersonal Sensitivity and Psychoticism contrib¬
uting no further prediction to BPII conversion. In the analy¬
sis based on self-reported usual personality, the three scores
measuring Mood Lability, Energy-Activity, and Daydream¬
ing significantly predicted BPII switching; Social Anxiety
did not contribute further to the prediction of BPII con¬
version. The agreement (concordance) between rank-
ordered predicted vs actual outcome (switching to BPII dis¬
order) was 79.6% for the three personality factor scores,
compared with 67.0% for the two self-reported symptom
scores and 64.4% for the two strongest clinical predictors.
In addition to overall goodness of fit, the logistic re¬
gression models can be evaluated in terms of their sen¬
sitivity or ability to correctly identify BPII converters (true
positives) vs their specificity or ability to correctly iden-
*Analysis of variance, covarying for age and sex. Data are given as
\MPI indicates Maudsley Personality Inventory23; GZTS,
Guilford-Zimmerman Temperament Survey25; MMPI, Minnesota Multiphasic
Personality Inventory29; LKA, Lazare-Klerman-Armor Personality Inventory
(short form)27; and IDI, Interpersonal Dependency Inventory.26
^Personality was assessed late in the index episode; cases are omitted if
switching occurred prior to such assessment (48-5=43).
tify patients with MDD who remain unipolar (true nega¬
tives) at different thresholds or cut points of predicted
value. At a high level of sensitivity (81.0%), the two best
clinical predictors and the two best self-described symp¬
tom scores each had low specificity (32.7% and 38.8%,
respectively). By comparison, prediction based on the
three new personality factor scores of Mood Lability, En¬
ergy-Activity, and Daydreaming attained a sensitivity level
of 90.7%, with a specificity of 44.9%. This high sensitiv¬
ity was achieved at the expense of a high rate of false posi¬
tives (55.1%). Given the low probability of BPII conver¬
sion in this sample (8.6%), the high sensitivity of
prediction from the newly derived personality self-
report measures provides a promising approach in iden-
tifiying persons at risk for converting to BPII disorder.
The highest specificity (86.2%) was achieved with Mood
Lability (all nine items), albeit with low sensitivity
Consistent with previous studies,5-6 including a more re¬
cent definitive report from the CDS,9 BPI conversion was
more likely from an acute, severe, psychotic depression
(even though the present analyses had excluded schi-
zoaffective patients), while BPII converters tended to pur¬
sue a course characterized by protracted depressive epi-
sodes with shorter well intervals.36 The present analyses
sought to characterize the temperamental instability pre¬
dictive of the latter course pattern.
Our major finding is the differential impact of "ha¬
bitual self" descriptions by clinically depressed patients
in predicting those who will ultimately switch to BPII.
Conceivably contaminated by state depression, these de¬
scriptors of presumably long-term trait characteristics—
Mood Lability, Energy-Activity, and Daydreaming—
nonetheless robustly distinguished them from both BPI
converters and those who remained unipolar for up to
11 years of prospective observation. The discussion that
follows addresses various méthodologie, conceptual, and
clinical issues relevant to BPII conversion.
Although family history for mania is a risk factor for
the development of BPI disorder,5·6·9 it makes relatively
little contribution in defining the BPII subtype.37 The Fam¬
ily History-RDC procedure38 used with each patient in
the CDS has no provision for recording periods of hy¬
pomania or even making inquiry for them. This is con¬
sidered problematic for the proband's personal history,
let alone for family members. Accordingly, the present
analyses did not examine this variable for BPII conver¬
The diagnosis of BPII is generally considered to be
unreliable,10 principally because hypomanic episodes can
easily be missed if the clinical interview is conducted by
nonexperts13·39 and when the patient is the sole source
of information. The first question, then, is whether some
or many patients characterized by these traits were al¬
ready BPII, but were misclassified at the index inter¬
view. This is plausible, given that some of the items de¬
scribing these traits have a hypomanic or cyclothymic ring
to them (Table 4, factors 1 and 2). Although more of the
BPII converters met RDC criteria for cyclothymia—
which suggests that hypomaniclike features perhaps were
experienced prior to prospectively observed switching—
they did not meet RDC criteria for BPII, which, by the
conventions of the state-of-the-art instrument we used
(ie, the SADS), requires major depressive and discrete hy¬
pomanic episodes. Nonetheless, because RDC cyclothy¬
mia occurred in only 8.3% of the BPII converters in the
present study, it cannot account for the personality mea¬
sures reported here. Cyclothymia according to RDC re¬
fers to alternating subsyndromal mood swings, lasting
for at least a few days in one or the other direction16 that
are often disruptive because of their intermittent chro-
nicity. The personality items reported in the present study
seem to fall below that threshold, both in terms of in¬
tensity and duration, perhaps occupying a position be¬
tween cyclothymia and normality. These consider¬
ations suggest that the conservative SADS conventions
for the diagnosis of BPII should be changed, permitting
the diagnosis of this affective subtype on the basis of tem¬
peramental dysregulation. Indeed, Cassano et al39 have
recently published data that support such liberalization
of the diagnostic threshold for this prevalent affective sub¬
type. Ultimately, it must be conceded that clinicians ex¬
perienced in diagnosing BPII will outperform research
assistants generating instrument-based diagnoses.12'13
Previously, the CDS has demonstrated state depen¬
dency for many personality measures.30 While some col-
*Based on factor analysis and analysis of variance on factor scores,
-\See Table 3 for explanations of abbreviations.
%For the sake of consistency, all pronouns were changed to the first
§False response adds +1.0 to factor.
oring of self-report by clinical depression is unavoid¬
able, it is unlikely to be the main explanation for the
findings reported herein. The self-report measures that
typified BPII converters (ie, Mood Lability, Energy Ac¬
tivity, and Daydreaming) were, in aggregate, uncharac¬
teristic of BPI converters and the majority ofpatients with
MDD who did not change polarity. If state influences were
strongly operative, moodiness and hyperenergetic activ¬
ity should have been blunted during BPII depression as
well. Moreover, the self-report temperamental traits in
BPII converters clustered in clinically meaningful
ways—as predicted from past research40—thereby sug¬
gesting that they do not represent spurious statistical find¬
We submit that BPII patients are unstable precisely
because their depressions are contaminated with their spe¬
cial temperament. In other words, the temperamental in¬
stability does not seem to go away during depressive epi¬
sodes.41 Observer-evaluated clinical data in this article
in support of such instability include the significantly
higher rate of antisocial behavior during the index epi¬
sode and history of educational and occupational mal¬
adjustment, drug use, and marital disruption in BPII con¬
verters as opposed to nonconverters. Finally, self-
reported traits indicative of Social Anxiety and
Daydreaming are cross-validated by clinician-observed
social withdrawal and self-rated symptom scores of in¬
terpersonal sensitivity and phobic anxiety. Again, this
clinical corroboration of the instability in BPII pro-
bands is itself strong evidence that the personality items
that characterized them are not spurious statistical arti¬
facts of a large number of analyses. Overall, these data
explain why BPII psychopathology and course have been
found to be highly variable, pleomorphic, and tempes¬
We next examine the question of whether switch¬
ing to BPII is related to unstable temperament or to some
specific consequence of it (eg, drug abuse, turmoil due
to marital disruption). Stepwise logistic regression analy¬
sis (data not shown) failed to reveal any predictive value
of drug use for BPII switching beyond that of marital dis¬
ruption. In a past contribution on cyclothymia, Akiskal
et al16 found that repeated romantic and marital failure
were characteristic; one third of their cyclothymic co¬
hort developed MDD during prospective course. Given
past NIMH research on the possible role of catechol-
aminergic excess and that of morning rapid eye move¬
ment sleep in the switch out of depression,1·2 it has been
hypothesized that sleep loss might be one of the medi¬
ating mechanisms in this process.43·44 Thus, the emo¬
tional turmoil and arousal associated with unstable ob¬
ject relationships might represent one of the putative
factors favoring switching to BPII and the generally tem¬
pestuous life course of this subtype.
Whether treatment with heterocyclic antidepres¬
sants favors a switch into hypomania or mania in vul¬
nerable individuals with bipolar diathesis5·616·45 cannot
be answered definitively with the methods of the pres¬
ent study. A case-control study and, ideally, a prospec¬
tive controlled trial of antidepressants vs placebo in BPII
are needed to resolve this question. The ideal study may
never be done because of ethical considerations regard-
*SADS indicates Schedule of Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia; RDC, Research Diagnostic Criteria; and SCL-90, Hopkins Symptom Checklist-90.
\Within each set of variables, no other predictor was added to the separation of groups once the braced predictors were entered into the regression
ing the induction of or acceleration into hypomania in
temperamentally unstable depressive patients. This fac¬
tor is probably operative in the present sample: Clini¬
cians must have judged that heterocyclic antidepres¬
sants were unsuitable for some patients—that indeed they
were at risk for switching—because only 28.9% of the
switchers as opposed to 67.7% of the nonswitcher uni¬
polar patients had received antidepressants. The fact that
many conversions, both into BPI and BPII, occurred dur¬
ing intake episodes or shortly thereafter is compatible with
the hypothesis that preswitching depressive patients are
more unstable and hence more likely to present for treat¬
ment and that the emotional turmoil surrounding affec¬
tive episodes, interacting in unknown ways with treat¬
ment parameters, could have facilitated switching.
It is striking that the temperamental profile of Mood
Lability described for BPII converters was absent in BPI
converters and those who remained unipolar. That these
habitual self measures were taken prior (often years be¬
fore) the actual conversion was observed, suggests "trait-
ness." Mood Lability itselfmight represent the very mecha¬
nism that underlies the ease with which switching into
hypomania occurs. "Switching," which connotes sud¬
denness, explains our terminologie preference for the pro¬
cess of BPII conversion. Hyperenergetic behavior (Energy-
Activity) has obvious hypomanic elements, while a rich
mental life (Daydreaming) captures the intense self-
absorption and active fantasy so characteristic of the de¬
ing is an active process that represents the mental
counterpart of hyperenergetic behavior, which is even
observed during depression. Thus, temperament and sub-
affective manifestations seem to merge in a continuum
of trait and state.40 The reported temperamental data on
switching supplement earlier CDS findings37-46 and those
by others47 on the distinctness of BPII.
The difficulties in the cross-sectional diagnosis of
BPII prior to switching appear to reflect their anteced¬
ent temperamental instability. Our data raise the possi¬
bility that such instability is so woven into the fluctuat¬
ing affective course of BPII that it constitutes a core
defining characteristic of this disorder, possibly sharing
the same pathophysiologic mechanisms.40 In other words,
Mood Lability appears to be an integral part of the affec¬
tive dysregulation that underlies BPII. Temperamental
excesses along these lines probably precede the onset of
full-blown affective disorder by a decade or longer; ac¬
tually, they have been identified in adolescents and chil¬
dren in clinical,48 epidemiologie,49 and high-risk50·51 bi¬
Mood Lability then appears as a stable trait across the
life span well into adulthood. Other investigators too have
commented on the tempestuous histories of BPII patients
compared with those with the BPI subtype.36·42·52 In the pres¬
ent study, this tempestuous affective course was associ¬
ated with significant interpersonal and vocational instabil¬
ity. These factors might account for the relative unreliability
(temporal instability),53 as well as the low sensitivity of cur-
rent diagnostic criteria for hypomania and BPII (leading
to underdiagnosis).12·13 Our newly derived personality fac¬
tors (Mood Lability, Energy-Activity, and Daydreaming)
raise the sensitivity of predicting who will switch to a re¬
spectable rate of 91%. If specificity is the desired goal of
the clinician, patient endorsement of all nine traits con-
situting Mood Lability provides a figure of 86%; however,
such specificity tends to drop the diagnostic sensitivity rate
With respect to Interpersonal Sensitivity, Phobic Ten¬
dencies, Daydreaming, and Miscellaneous Psychopathol¬
ogy beyond the usual affective realm, and especially for
its propensity to Mood Lability, BPII overlaps consider¬
ably with the constructs ofhysteroid dysphoria and atypi¬
cal depression.54·55 This is of great therapeutic rel¬
evance, since both atypical depressive and BPII patients
respond to monoamine oxidase inhibitors and, in at least
two studies,56 57 have been found to do significantly less
well with tricyclic antidepressants.
The most important clinical implication of our data,
however, resides in the fact that the unstable tempera¬
mental profile that presaged switching to BPII was ob¬
tained during antecedent clinical depression, ie, at a time
when practitioners normally have access to personality
data. Our findings are difficult to translate to DSM-IVs
Axis II.58 However,judging from Axis II diagnoses given
to cyclothymic and BPII patients,'6·59 patients with MDD
who meet erratic or cluster  criteria (especially border¬
line and histrionic) at the trait level—concurrently with
shy-sensitive traits of the avoidant type from cluster
C—should be suspected as belonging to the BPII sub¬
We submit that it is the combined restless emotion¬
ality of cluster  and the introverted neuroticism of clus¬
ter C that contributes to the special instability of BPII.
This combination seems to be an incompatible align¬
ment of temperamental traits that often proves highly dis¬
tressing to the sufferer (and significant others). That may
be why patients with MDD having such features are li¬
able to switching. Actually, emotional lability and hy-
perenergetic activity during a major depressive episode
suggest that BPII is, in a certain measure, a mixed epi¬
sode.41 On a more hypothetical level, the emotional storms
of these patients might teleologically represent attempts
for self-cure from depressive inertia, affect, and self-
absorption; hence, their erratic behavior, which might
even be misconstrued as psychopathic.14·60 Temperamen¬
tal instability, coupled with the miscellaneous psycho-
pathologic symptoms beyond that which is considered
typical of depression, explains why such patients often
are diagnosed as borderline, thereby obscuring the af¬
fective origin of the psychopathology.
Bipolar II represents the most fascinating interface
between affective episodes and temperamental instabil¬
ity. Indeed, it might be the most common phenotype of
bipolar disorder.12,39-43·61 Although there is some possi¬
bility that it might breed true37 in terms of family mor¬
bidity rates for full-blown bipolar illness, it is interme¬
diate between BPI and strict unipolar disorder.47·39 Despite
some division of opinion in the research literature,62 ear¬
lier suggestions by Dunner et al,42 supported by us and
others,43·46·63·64 have finally led to its inclusion into DSM-
IV58 as a bipolar subtype. Given that it responds to treat¬
ment with lithium salts,65·66 clinicians will now have to
struggle to make sense of this interface between soft bi-
polarity and Axis II. As documented elsewhere,16"18 tem-
peramentality along cyclothymic and hyperthymic lines—
examined longitudinally—constitutes genuine affective
dysregulation distinct from psychopathy. In particular,
switching to hypomania does not occur in the antisocial
personality spectrum (including Briquet's disorder).16 In
a subsequent article, we will examine to what extent the
temperamental traits predictive of BPII switching match
the temperamental profile of an unselected sample of pa¬
